medical_history.chief_complaint: Micrognathia and severely restricted mouth opening
medical_history.history_of_present_illness: At 3 days of age the infant was transferred for ongoing management. Nasogastric feeding was commenced and elective tracheostomy performed on the fifth day of life for marked desaturations while in the prone position.
medical_history.past_medical_history: Karyotyping, echocardiogram, head ultrasound, renal ultrasound and EEG were normal. Facial CT demonstrated a symmetrical but hypoplastic mandible, with obtuse mandibular angles and normal mandibular condyles.
medical_history.family_history: The parents were unrelated but the mother and maternal relatives had phenotypes consistent with VWS.

physical_examination.general_examination: Apgar scores at 1 and 5 minutes were 5 and 9, respectively. Birth weight was 3300 g (53rd centile), length 49 cm (50th centile), and head circumference 32 cm (<10th centile). Micrognathia and severely restricted mouth opening was noted on physical examination; the maximum intergum distance was 2 mm.
physical_examination.specialty_examination: Examination under anesthesia (EUA) was performed at 1 month of age. There was no improvement in mandibular mobility with muscle relaxant, and no cause was found for the rigid obstruction to mouth opening. Over subsequent weeks intraoral adhesions between the maxilla and mandible became evident on examination.

radiographic_examinations.findings: Symmetrical but hypoplastic mandible, with obtuse mandibular angles and normal mandibular condyles. Bony defect of the hard palate and a defect of the soft palate
Unremarkable, demonstrating no evidence of masseteric or temporalis scarring or banding

other_tests.findings: No improvement in mandibular mobility with muscle relaxant, and no cause was found for the rigid obstruction to mouth opening

other_tests.procedure_name: EUA and lysis of intraoral adhesions
other_tests.findings: Mouth opening was 3 mm preoperatively and 6 mm following diathermy division of the soft tissue adhesions. The masseter muscles were tight bilaterally. The floor of mouth was abnormal, and the tongue was posteriorly displaced and protruding into the nasopharynx above the palate
other_tests.changes_over_time: At review 8 weeks later, the infant had a 30-mm interalveolar opening

other_tests.procedure_name: Tongue base advancement with a circummandibular Prolene suture
other_tests.findings: Oral intake increased to the extent that nasogastric feeding was able to be ceased
other_tests.changes_over_time: The patient underwent successful elective decannulation of his tracheostomy at 12 months of age. The circummandibular suture was removed and the tongue remained in an anatomically normal position. Mouth opening is now normal
No further difficulties with either feeding or airway
